title: Fathers and faith formation | Institute for Family Studies
gist: A teaser for the IFS/Communio report 'Passing the Torch' arguing fathers exert outsized, under-appreciated influence over whether their children retain faith into adulthood, against a backdrop of double-digit declines in U.S. religious participation since the 1990s driven by generational replacement.

insights.claim: U.S. religious participation has fallen by nearly every measure (church attendance, belief in God, self-reported faith) since the 1990s, and the decline is largely generational, with each new cohort entering adulthood less religious than the prior one.
insights.speaker: Jane Lankes Smith, Jesse Smith
insights.so_what: A generational (not life-cycle) decline compounds over time, signaling structural shrinkage of the religious-affiliated consumer and donor base rather than a recoverable dip.
insights.quote: religious participation in America has fallen by nearly every measure (church attendance, belief in God, self-reported faith, etc.) since the 1990s
insights.locator: The report addresses a sobering trend

insights.claim: Fathers carry far greater influence over whether their children grow up to share their faith than they typically realize, per the IFS/Communio report 'Passing the Torch: How Faith Moves Across Generations.'
insights.speaker: Jane Lankes Smith, Jesse Smith
insights.so_what: Faith-formation interventions aimed at fathers specifically may be the highest-leverage point for religious institutions trying to slow attrition.
insights.quote: fathers carry an influence far greater than they often realize
insights.locator: Our recent report with the Institute for Family Studies and Communio
